title: Clay swirl cold-process soap
subtitle: A two-colour in-the-pot swirl using white kaolin and charcoal-tinted batter, on an olive, coconut, and castor base
excerpt: In-the-pot swirl is a cold-process soap technique that produces a marbled pattern throughout the bar. The base batter is divided at trace, one half coloured with fine charcoal, and the two batters are swirled together in the mould with a skewer or chopstick. The result varies each time, making every batch of bars distinct.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat; 33% lye discount. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997). In-the-pot swirl technique: Bramble Berry soap design guide (2023). Activated charcoal as a soap colourant: cosmetic grade; does not affect saponification. White kaolin clay dispersed in oil: standard technique for clay additives.

recipe.batchNotes: Recalculate lye weight for any oil changes. Clays and charcoal are additives; they do not affect the lye calculation.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 2 days in the mould before unmoulding. Minimum 4-week cure; 6 weeks for a harder bar.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years in a cool, dark place on an open rack. Discard any bar with orange spots (DOS) or a paint-like smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point at which the soap batter has thickened to a custard-like consistency. For swirl work, a very light trace allows the batters to flow and swirl together before setting.
The percentage of oil left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5%, the bar is conditioning and mild.
The reaction between fat and lye that produces soap and glycerine. The reaction continues in the mould for the first 24 to 48 hours.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut to reveal the swirl
projectSchedule.body: Unmould after 2 days. The swirl pattern is revealed when you cut through the loaf. Cut wearing protective gloves.

projectSchedule.title: Four-week check
projectSchedule.body: Test a sliver on skin. Ready to use.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: Fully cured, hard, and stable for two years.

content.text: In-the-pot swirl is a cold-process soap technique where two differently coloured batters are combined in the mould at
trace

and swirled with a skewer or thin spatula to produce a marbled pattern throughout the bar. This version divides the base batter into two halves at light trace: one half receives white kaolin clay for a cream tone, the other receives activated charcoal for a dark grey tone. The two batters are poured alternately into the mould and swirled to create a pattern that is revealed when the loaf is sliced. No two batches swirl identically.
The oil blend is 65% olive, 30% coconut, and 5% castor. At 5%
superfat
and 33% lye discount, the bar is mild and conditioning. Work quickly at a very light trace; if the batters thicken too far before swirling, the pattern will not develop.
Put on protective gloves and goggles before starting.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: 30% of oil weight; melt if solid
Dispersed in 1 tablespoon of olive oil for the light batter
Dispersed in 1 tablespoon of olive oil for the dark batter

content.text: Method
Prepare the colour slurries

Mix the white kaolin clay into 1 tablespoon of olive oil in one small bowl. Mix the activated charcoal into 1 tablespoon of olive oil in a second small bowl. Both should be smooth and lump-free. Set aside.
Mix the lye solution

In a ventilated space with goggles and protective gloves on, weigh the lye separately. Pour distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Add lye to water in a steady stream; never reverse. The solution heats quickly; stand back. Cool to 38 to 42°C.
Blend to very light trace and divide

Combine all oils in a heat-safe bowl. Cool to 38 to 42°C. Pour the lye solution into the oils. Stick-blend in very short bursts (2 seconds each), stirring between, until the batter is just at emulsion stage, thin as warm milk. Divide the batter equally between two pitchers or bowls.
Colour and combine

Stir the kaolin slurry into one half and blend briefly. Stir the charcoal slurry into the other half and blend briefly. Both batters should remain thin and pourable. Pour them alternately into the mould in thin streams, overlapping as you go. Drag a skewer through the mould from side to side in a zigzag to create the swirl. The
saponification
reaction will set the pattern as it progresses. Wrap the mould in a towel and leave for 2 days.
Unmould, cut, and cure
Unmould after 2 days. The swirl pattern is revealed as you slice through the loaf. Cut wearing protective gloves. Cure on an open rack for 4 to 6 weeks.
Storage and shelf life
Two years in a cool, dark place on an open rack. Discard on signs of DOS or a paint-like smell.
